title: Session 2: CRUD, HTTP Methods & URL Anatomy
text:
In Session 1, we established the conceptual foundation:

APIs are interfaces for software communication
Data flows: Client → API → Server → Database (and back)
Responses come as JSON (key-value pairs, nested structures)
Status codes tell us if the request succeeded (200) or not (4xx, 5xx)

Now we’re going to put that knowledge into action — understanding how we interact with APIs and what those interactions look like in code.

text:
In Session 1, we established the conceptual foundation:

Responses come as JSON (key-value pairs, nested structures)

title: Session 2: CRUD, HTTP Methods & URL Anatomy
text:
Part 1: The CRUD Framework
What type of client requests can we make? The CRUD framework organizes the four fundamental operations:



CRUD Operation
HTTP Method
Description




Create
POST
Submit new data to the server


Read
GET
Retrieve existing data from the server


Update
PUT / PATCH
Modify existing data on the server


Delete
DELETE
Remove a resource from the server



The image below maps each CRUD operation to a visual icon: a plus sign for Create/POST (adding something new), an eye for Read/GET (looking at existing data), a pencil for Update/PUT-PATCH (editing something), and a trash can for Delete/DELETE (removing it). As a data scientist, the eye (Read/GET) quadrant will cover the vast majority of your API work.







NoteFor Data Science



In data science, Read (GET) is by far the most common operation. We’re typically pulling data, not writing to databases. However, POST is sometimes needed — for example, to submit credentials and receive an access token.



———————


Visual Check — CRUD Operations.
(Multiple Choice): A social media app lets users edit their display name. Based on the image above, which CRUD operation and HTTP method does that action map to?

Create — POST
Read — GET
Update — PUT/PATCH
Delete — DELETE







TipAnswer — Visual Check





c) Update — PUT/PATCH. Editing an existing record (changing a name that already exists) is an Update operation. PUT replaces the entire resource; PATCH modifies only the changed fields. Either could apply here, but the key point is that this is not Create (nothing new is being added) or Read (data is being changed, not just viewed).




———————


———————



Q1.
(Multiple Choice): Which CRUD operation is most commonly used in data science when working with APIs?

Create
Read
Update
Delete







TipAnswer Q1





b) Read. Data scientists most commonly use GET requests to read (retrieve) data from external sources. We’re consumers of data, not typically writing to external databases.




———————


———————



Q2.
(Open-Ended — Callback to Session 1, Q4): In Session 1, we learned that a response contains data, metadata, and a status code. Now, using the CRUD framework, explain: when you perform a Read operation, what HTTP method do you use, and what would a successful response look like?






TipAnswer Q2





A Read operation uses the GET HTTP method. A successful response would have a 200 OK status code, and the body would contain JSON data with the key-value pairs we requested — for example, "temperature": 72 or "city": "San Luis Obispo".




———————

title: Session 2: CRUD, HTTP Methods & URL Anatomy
text:
Part 2: GET Requests
A GET request is all about retrieving data. You’re asking the server: “Give me this information.”
The animation below shows a GET request for the resource GET /users/42. Watch how the request originates at the Client and travels through the API to the Server and Database. Notice two things: first, the request carries only a path (/users/42) — no data payload. Second, the response brings data back to the client while nothing new is written to the database.

Here is the flow of a GET request:

Client constructs a request for a resource.
API receives and validates the client’s request.
Server locates the requested data within the database.
Client receives the requested data from the server.







NoteGET Characteristics




The request parameters are encoded in the URL (visible in the address bar)
GET requests are meant to be read-only — they don’t change data on the server
They are idempotent — making the same request multiple times gives the same result




———————


Visual Check — GET Request.
(Open-Ended): The animation shows a request for GET /users/42. If you changed the request to GET /users/7, what part of the request changes and what stays exactly the same?






TipAnswer — Visual Check





The path parameter changes from 42 to 7 — you’re now asking for a different user record. Everything else stays the same: the HTTP method is still GET, the endpoint path structure (/users/) is the same, and the overall flow (client → API → server → database → back) is identical. Only the specific resource being requested changes.




———————


———————



Q3.
(Open-Ended): Describe another example of a task a data scientist might perform using a GET request, beyond retrieving weather data.






TipAnswer Q3





A data scientist could use a GET request to retrieve a list of the top 10 most popular articles from the New York Times API, fetch historical stock price data from a financial API, or get public information about a specific user from the GitHub API.




———————

title: Session 2: CRUD, HTTP Methods & URL Anatomy
text:
Part 3: POST Requests
A POST request is about sending new data to the server. You’re telling the server: “Here’s something new — store it.”
The animation below shows a POST request sending POST /users with a JSON payload { "name": "Maya", "email": "maya@example.com" }. Compare this carefully to the GET animation: with GET, data flowed from the server to the client. With POST, data originates at the client and moves toward the server. The client is the source; the server is the destination.

Here is the flow of a POST request:

Client sends new data within the request body.
API receives and validates the client’s new data.
Server creates a new record in the database.
Client receives a confirmation for the new record.







ImportantGET vs. POST — Key Difference




GET sends parameters in the URL — you’re asking for data.
POST sends data in the request body — you’re submitting new data.

Think of it this way: GET is reading a book from the library. POST is donating a new book to the library.



———————


Visual Check — POST Request.
(Multiple Choice): In the POST animation, the JSON payload { "name": "Maya", "email": "maya@example.com" } travels from the Client to the Server. Where does this data live in the request, and how is that different from a GET request?

It is in the URL query string, just like GET parameters.
It is in the request body — unlike GET, which puts parameters in the URL.
It is sent as a separate file attachment.
It is stored in the API and forwarded automatically — no client action needed.







TipAnswer — Visual Check





b) POST sends data in the request body — a separate payload attached to the request. GET requests have no body; all their parameters are encoded directly in the URL (visible in the address bar). This is why POST is used for sending sensitive or large data — it keeps the payload out of the URL.




———————


———————



Q4.
(Multiple Choice): Which of the following data science tasks would most likely require a POST request instead of a GET request?

Retrieving the current temperature for a city.
Getting a list of your public repositories from the GitHub API.
Submitting a new tweet through the X (formerly Twitter) API.
Looking up the coordinates for a specific address.







TipAnswer Q4





c) Submitting a new tweet involves creating a new resource on the server, which is the primary purpose of a POST request. The other options involve only retrieving existing data.




———————


———————



Q5.
(Open-Ended — Callback to Q1): We said POST maps to the Create operation in CRUD. In the restaurant analogy from Session 1, if a GET request is like reading the menu and placing an order, what would a POST request be analogous to?






TipAnswer Q5





A POST request would be like submitting a new recipe to the restaurant’s kitchen — you’re not reading what’s already there, you’re adding something new to their collection. Or, you could think of it as filling out a comment card that gets filed in the restaurant’s records.




———————

title: Session 2: CRUD, HTTP Methods & URL Anatomy
text:
Part 4: URL Anatomy
So how do we actually construct a request? A request is defined by a URL, which contains both:

The endpoint (base address of the API) — the stable part
The query string (key-value pairs that customize the request) — the dynamic part

The animation below shows the Client (left) and API Server (right) with the URL being broken down between them. It highlights how the full URL carries everything the server needs — protocol, host, endpoint path, and each query parameter — in a single string. Pay attention to where the endpoint ends and where the query string begins.


Breaking Down a URL
Consider this example URL:
https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather?q=San+Luis+Obispo&appid=YOUR_KEY&units=imperial



Component
Value
Purpose




Protocol
https://
Secure communication


Base URL
api.openweathermap.org
The API server


Endpoint
/data/2.5/weather
Which resource you want


Query separator
?
Marks the start of parameters


Parameter 1
q=San+Luis+Obispo
City name (URL-encoded)


Parameter separator
&
Separates key-value pairs


Parameter 2
appid=YOUR_KEY
Your API key


Parameter 3
units=imperial
Unit preference









NoteKey Vocabulary




An endpoint is the specific URL where an API resource can be accessed. It’s the stable part of the URL that doesn’t change from one request to the next.
A query string customizes the request using key-value pairs. It always starts with ? and pairs are separated by &.




———————



Visual Check — URL Anatomy.
(Open-Ended): Looking at the animation and the URL breakdown above, what single character marks the boundary between the endpoint and the query string? And if you accidentally left out your API key parameter entirely, which HTTP status code from Session 1 would you most likely receive back?






TipAnswer — Visual Check





The ? character marks the end of the endpoint and the start of the query string. Everything before ? is the stable address of the resource; everything after it is customization. If you omit your API key, the server cannot verify who you are — you would most likely receive a 401 Unauthorized status code, since authentication is missing.




———————


———————



Q6.
(Open-Ended): In the URL https://api.spotify.com/v1/search?q=Daft+Punk&type=artist, identify the endpoint and the two key-value pairs that make up the query string.






TipAnswer Q6






Endpoint: https://api.spotify.com/v1/search
Key-Value Pairs: q=Daft+Punk and type=artist





———————


———————



Q7.
(Multiple Choice — Callback to Session 1 Q4): When your R script makes a GET request to the OpenWeather API, which statement is correct?

Your script sends JSON and receives a URL.
Your script sends a URL and receives JSON.
Your script sends JSON and receives JSON.
Your script sends a URL and receives a URL.







TipAnswer Q7





b) The request itself is a carefully constructed URL containing the endpoint and query parameters. The API’s response comes back as JSON containing the data, metadata, and status code.




———————

title: Session 2: CRUD, HTTP Methods & URL Anatomy
section: Part 5: Under the Hood — What Happens When You Make a Request
text:
Part 5: Under the Hood — What Happens When You Make a Request
Let’s connect everything together:

You construct a URL from a base URL (endpoint) + query string (parameters).
Your client sends a GET request using this URL to the API.
The API relays this request to the server.
The server processes the request, queries the database, and sends a response.
The response includes a status code and the requested data (formatted as JSON).
Your R code parses the JSON into a data structure you can work with.







ImportantCommon Misconception



We are not sending JSON when we make a GET request. We’re sending a URL with parameters. JSON is what comes back to us in the response.



———————


Q8.
(Open-Ended): Put the following steps in the correct order for making an API request in R:

Parse the JSON response into a data frame
Construct the URL with endpoint and query parameters
Check the status code for success (200)
Send the request using req_perform()
Create a request object with request()







TipAnswer Q8






Construct the URL with endpoint and query parameters
Create a request object with request()
Send the request using req_perform()
Check the status code for success (200)
Parse the JSON response into a data frame





———————

title: Session 2: CRUD, HTTP Methods & URL Anatomy
section: Part 6: Hands-On — Making Requests to a Dummy Server
text:
Part 6: Hands-On — Making Requests to a Dummy Server
Now let’s get our hands dirty! We’ll make real requests to a practice server to see how this works before moving to the Weather API tomorrow.

Load Libraries

library(httr2)       # Makes web requests
library(tibble)      # Tidyverse data frames
library(dplyr)       # Data manipulation
library(glue)        # String construction



Making a GET Request
Let’s start with a simple GET request to a public test API:

# Step 1: Define the URL
test_url <- "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1"

# Step 2: Create a request object
req <- request(test_url)

# Step 3: Perform the request
response <- req_perform(req)

# Step 4: Check the status code
resp_status(response)

# Step 5: Check the content type
resp_content_type(response)


# Step 6: Parse the JSON response
result <- resp_body_json(response)

# Step 7: Inspect the structure
str(result)


# Step 8: Convert to a data frame
user_df <- as.data.frame(result)
print(user_df)



Making a GET Request with Query Parameters

# Get posts by a specific user
posts_url <- "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts"

# Method 1: Using glue to build the URL
posts_url_query <- glue("{posts_url}?userId=1")

req <- request(posts_url_query)
response <- req_perform(req)

if (resp_status(response) == 200) {
  posts <- resp_body_json(response)
  cat("Number of posts retrieved:", length(posts), "\n")
}



Making a POST Request
Now let’s send data to the server:

# Step 1: Define the endpoint
post_url <- "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts"

# Step 2: Create the request with a body
req <- request(post_url) |>
  req_method("POST") |>
  req_headers("Content-Type" = "application/json") |>
  req_body_json(list(
    title = "My API Workshop Post",
    body = "Learning about APIs is fun!",
    userId = 1
  ))

# Step 3: Perform the request
response <- req_perform(req)

# Step 4: Check the status code (201 = Created)
cat("Status:", resp_status(response), "\n")

# Step 5: See the response
result <- resp_body_json(response)
print(result)







NoteStatus Code 201



Notice we got a 201 Created instead of 200 OK. This is the standard success code for POST requests — it means the server successfully created the new resource.



———————



Q9.
(Open-Ended — Callback to Q7): In the GET request to the dummy server, we sent a URL and received JSON. In the POST request, we sent JSON in the request body. How does this change what we learned about GET vs. POST?






TipAnswer Q9





This reinforces the key difference: GET requests encode parameters in the URL and receive JSON back. POST requests send data in the request body (often as JSON) and receive a confirmation (also JSON) back. So POST actually involves JSON going both directions, while GET only involves JSON coming back.




———————

title: Session 1: JSON Fundamentals & Request/Response
text:
Part 1: What is an API?
API stands for Application Programming Interface. At its core, an API is a way for software to communicate with other software. In data science, we use APIs to programmatically request data from external servers — weather services, sports databases, social media platforms, and more.
Think of the restaurant analogy:

You (the client) look at the menu and place an order.
The waiter (the API) carries your order to the kitchen.
The kitchen (the server/database) prepares your food.
The waiter brings back your meal (the response).

The key insight: you never walk into the kitchen yourself. The API is the controlled interface that lets you interact with the data source.

———————


Q1.
(Multiple Choice): What is the primary utility of a web API for a data analyst?

To create visual websites for data projects.
To allow programmatic, repeatable, and direct access to live data from an external source.
To store large CSV files in the cloud.
To design the database structure for a server.







TipAnswer Q1





b) An API provides a structured way for a program (like an R script) to directly request and receive live data, making data acquisition automated and scalable.




———————

title: Session 1: JSON Fundamentals & Request/Response
text:
Part 2: API Data Flow
Let’s go deeper into the architecture. The flow of data through an API follows a predictable pattern:
Request direction: Client (request) → API → Server → Database
Response direction: Client ← API ← Server (response) ← Database
The animation below traces a single API call from start to finish. There are four components in the chain: the Client (your R script or browser), the API (the endpoint — the “front door”), the Server (backend logic that processes the request), and the Database (where the actual data lives). The request moves left to right; the response travels the same path in reverse.







NoteKey Observations




The client is your R script or browser — anything making the request.
The API is the endpoint — the “front door” of the data service.
The server processes the request and queries the database.
The response travels back the same path.
A client and server can exist on the same computer — this is common in local development.




———————


Visual Check — API Data Flow.
(Multiple Choice): In the animation, the API sits between the Client and the Server. What best describes the API’s role in this chain?

It stores the data permanently.
It acts as a gateway — receiving requests from the client and routing them to the server.
It runs the backend logic that queries the database.
It is the same as the database.







TipAnswer — Visual Check





b) The API is a gateway or controlled interface. It does not store data (that’s the database) and it does not run the core business logic (that’s the server). It is the stable “front door” that clients talk to, and it routes requests onward.




———————


———————



Q2.
(Open-Ended): Using the restaurant analogy where the API is a waiter, describe the step-by-step process of you (the client) successfully ordering and receiving a “current weather forecast” from the kitchen (the server).






TipAnswer Q2





I (the client) look at the menu (API documentation) and decide what I want. I then give my specific order for the “current weather forecast” to the waiter (the API). The waiter takes my request to the kitchen (the server), which has access to the fridge (the database). The kitchen prepares my order and gives the finished dish (the JSON data response) back to the waiter, who delivers it to my table.




———————


———————



Q3.
(Multiple Choice — Callback to Q1): In the restaurant analogy, the API documentation is most analogous to:

The kitchen equipment
The menu
The cash register
The table number







TipAnswer Q3





b) The menu. Just like a menu tells you what’s available and how to order it, API documentation tells you what endpoints exist, what parameters you can send, and what responses to expect.




———————

title: Session 1: JSON Fundamentals & Request/Response
text:
Part 3: Requests and Responses
Now let’s zoom in on the two key actions: requests and responses.

What is a Request?
The client sends a request asking for information. This request includes:

A URL (e.g., with parameters like ?q=San+Luis+Obispo)
Possibly an API key (for authentication)
A method (e.g., GET or POST — more on these in Session 2)

The request is constructed as a URL string. We’ll dissect URL anatomy in the next session.


What is a Response?
The server returns a response which contains:

Data — the actual information you requested (temperature, artist name, forecast, etc.)
Metadata — information about the response (timestamps, rate limits, etc.)
Status code — tells you whether the request was successful (e.g., 200 OK)

This information is traditionally provided in JSON format.

———————



Q4.
(Open-Ended): What is the fundamental difference between an API request and an API response?






TipAnswer Q4





A request is what you send to a server to ask for information. It’s an outgoing message that includes the endpoint URL, your API key, and the specific parameters for your query. A response is what the server sends back to you. It’s an incoming message containing the data you asked for (the body), a status code indicating success or failure, and other metadata.




———————

title: Session 1: JSON Fundamentals & Request/Response
section: Part 4: Anatomy of a JSON Response
text:
Part 4: Anatomy of a JSON Response
Now let’s focus on the response — specifically, what it looks like. API responses are almost always formatted as JSON (JavaScript Object Notation).
The animation below reveals the layers of a JSON response. The entire thing — everything the server sends back — is called the Body or Content. It is wrapped in curly braces { }. Inside that body, the content is divided into three zones: the data (what you asked for), metadata (context about the response), and the status code (the success or failure signal). The animation builds these zones one at a time so you can see how they fit together.







NoteWhat is JSON?



When we send a request to an API, we get a response body, which includes the content — typically JSON — divided into:

Data: what we actually wanted (temperature, city name, coordinates)
Metadata: information about the response (timestamps, units, request ID)
Status code: tells us if the request worked




———————


Visual Check — JSON Response Anatomy.
(Open-Ended): The animation shows the entire response wrapped in { }. When you call resp_body_json() in R to parse this response, what R data structure does that outer { } (a JSON object) become, and how would you access the value for a key called "temperature" inside it?






TipAnswer — Visual Check





A JSON object ({ }) becomes a named list in R. To access the value for "temperature", you would use result$temperature or result[["temperature"]], where result is the variable holding the parsed response.




———————



JSON Structure: Key-Value Pairs
JSON organizes data using key-value pairs. Here’s a simple example:
{
  "city": "San Luis Obispo",
  "temperature": 72,
  "units": "imperial",
  "conditions": "clear sky"
}
Key features of JSON:

Data is wrapped in curly braces { }
Each entry is a "key": value pair
Values can be strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, or nested objects
Nested objects are JSON inside JSON — like a list within a list



Nested JSON Example
{
  "city": "San Luis Obispo",
  "coordinates": {
    "lat": 35.28,
    "lon": -120.66
  },
  "weather": {
    "main": "Clear",
    "temp": 72,
    "humidity": 45
  }
}






ImportantThinking in R Terms



In R, a JSON response becomes a named list — potentially containing other named lists inside it. This is what we mean by “nested.” When we flatten it with as.data.frame(), nested keys become column names like coordinates.lat or weather.temp.



———————



Q5.
(Multiple Choice): The JSON data format, commonly used in API responses, organizes information using what fundamental structure?

Rows and columns
A nested series of bullet points
Key-value pairs
Formatted paragraphs of text







TipAnswer Q5





c) JSON data is built on key-value pairs (e.g., "city": "Chicago"), where a “key” is a string and a “value” can be a string, number, boolean, array, or another object.




———————


———————



Q6.
(Open-Ended — Callback to Q4): Given the nested JSON example above, if you converted this to a data frame in R using as.data.frame(), what would the column name for the latitude value be?






TipAnswer Q6





The column name would be coordinates.lat. When R flattens a nested JSON structure into a data frame, it joins the nested key names with a period. So coordinates → lat becomes coordinates.lat.




———————

title: Session 1: JSON Fundamentals & Request/Response
text:
Part 5: Status Codes
Status codes tell you what happened with your request. They are grouped by category:



Range
Category
Meaning




1xx
Informational
Request received, processing…


2xx
Success
Request was successful


3xx
Redirect
Resource moved to a different URL


4xx
Client Error
Something wrong with your request


5xx
Server Error
Something wrong on the server’s end



The most important ones for data work:

200 OK — Success! You got your data.
401 Unauthorized — Your API key is missing or invalid.
403 Forbidden — You don’t have permission to access this resource.
404 Not Found — The endpoint or resource doesn’t exist.
429 Too Many Requests — You’ve hit the rate limit.
500 Internal Server Error — The server broke; not your fault.







NotePractical Tip



In most data API work, your goal is to get a 200 response. If you don’t, the status code tells you where to look for the problem — your request (4xx) or their server (5xx).



———————


Q7.
(Discussion): You write a script to get weather data, but it fails with a 404 Not Found status code. Based on the 400-level error category, is it more likely that the weather company’s server is broken, or that you made a mistake? What part of your request is the most likely source of the error?






TipAnswer Q7





A 404 Not Found error is a client-side error (4xx), meaning the mistake is on my end. It is not a server error (5xx). The most likely problem is that the URL endpoint I used in my request is incorrect or misspelled — I’ve asked for a “page” that doesn’t exist on the server.




———————


———————



Q8.
(Multiple Choice — Callback to Q5): If you receive a response with a status code of 200 and the body contains JSON, which of the following would you expect to find in that response?

An error message explaining what went wrong
Key-value pairs containing the data you requested
A redirect URL to a different API
An empty response with no content







TipAnswer Q8





b) A 200 OK status means success, and the JSON body will contain the key-value pairs with the data you requested — temperature, coordinates, city name, etc.




———————


———————



Q9.
(Open-Ended): You receive a 401 Unauthorized error when trying to access weather data. You’ve double-checked the URL and it’s correct. What is the most likely cause, and how would you fix it?






TipAnswer Q9





A 401 Unauthorized error means your API key is missing, invalid, or expired. The fix is to check that you’ve properly loaded your API key (e.g., from your .Renviron file), that the key is still active on the provider’s website, and that you’re passing it correctly in the request URL or headers.




———————

title: Session 1: JSON Fundamentals & Request/Response
text:
Session 1 Recap
In this session, we covered the conceptual foundation for working with APIs:

What an API is — a controlled interface for software-to-software communication
API data flow — Client → API → Server → Database (and back)
Requests vs. Responses — what you send vs. what you get back
JSON format — key-value pairs, nesting, and how it maps to R data structures
Status codes — how to diagnose what happened with your request

Up next in Session 2: We’ll learn about CRUD operations, HTTP methods (GET vs. POST), URL anatomy, and make our first requests to a dummy server!

title: Practice Lab 5: GitHub REST API
text:
The GitHub REST API is the most realistic example in this workshop — it is a production API used by millions of developers daily. It introduces two concepts you haven’t seen yet:

No-auth public access — many endpoints work without any credential, though with strict rate limits
Fine-grained Personal Access Tokens (PATs) — scoped tokens where you explicitly choose what permissions to grant

The PAT exercise is a live demonstration of least-privilege security: only give a credential exactly the access it needs, and nothing more.
Auth patterns: None (public) → Bearer token with Authorization: Bearer TOKEN.

title: Practice Lab 5: GitHub REST API
section: Step 1 — No Signup Needed for Public Data
text:
Step 1 — No Signup Needed for Public Data
GitHub’s public endpoints work immediately. No account or key required for the first two exercises.

library(httr2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(glue)
library(dotenv)

title: Practice Lab 5: GitHub REST API
section: Exercise 1: Retrieve Public Repository Data (No Auth)
text:
Exercise 1: Retrieve Public Repository Data (No Auth)

# GitHub requires a User-Agent header — always include it
req <- request("https://api.github.com/users/hadley/repos") |>
  req_headers(
    Accept       = "application/vnd.github+json",
    `User-Agent` = "API-Workshop-Student"
  ) |>
  req_url_query(per_page = 30, sort = "updated")

response <- req_perform(req)

cat("Status:", resp_status(response), "\n")
cat("Rate limit remaining:", resp_header(response, "x-ratelimit-remaining"), "\n")


repos <- resp_body_json(response)

repos_df <- lapply(repos, function(r) {
  data.frame(
    name            = r$name,
    description     = substr(r$description %||% "", 1, 80),
    language        = r$language %||% NA_character_,
    stars           = r$stargazers_count,
    forks           = r$forks_count,
    open_issues     = r$open_issues_count,
    is_fork         = r$fork,
    created_at      = r$created_at,
    updated_at      = r$updated_at
  )
}) |> bind_rows()

repos_df |>
  arrange(desc(stars)) |>
  select(name, language, stars, forks) |>
  head(10)







NoteRate Limits Without Auth



Unauthenticated requests are limited to 60 per hour (shared by your IP address). The x-ratelimit-remaining header tells you how many you have left. With a PAT, the limit rises to 5,000 per hour.

title: Practice Lab 5: GitHub REST API
text:
Exercise 2: Search Repositories
GitHub’s search API lets you find repositories by topic, language, stars, and more.

req <- request("https://api.github.com/search/repositories") |>
  req_headers(
    Accept       = "application/vnd.github+json",
    `User-Agent` = "API-Workshop-Student"
  ) |>
  req_url_query(
    q        = "topic:tidyverse language:R",
    sort     = "stars",
    order    = "desc",
    per_page = 20
  )

response    <- req_perform(req)
search_data <- resp_body_json(response)

cat("Total matching repositories:", search_data$total_count, "\n")


search_df <- lapply(search_data$items, function(r) {
  data.frame(
    name        = r$full_name,
    description = substr(r$description %||% "", 1, 80),
    language    = r$language %||% NA_character_,
    stars       = r$stargazers_count,
    forks       = r$forks_count,
    topics      = paste(unlist(r$topics), collapse = ", ")
  )
}) |> bind_rows()

search_df |> arrange(desc(stars)) |> head(10)

title: Practice Lab 5: GitHub REST API
section: Exercise 3: Create a Fine-Grained Personal Access Token
text:
Exercise 3: Create a Fine-Grained Personal Access Token

Step 3a — Create the Token

Go to github.com → your avatar → Settings
Scroll to Developer Settings → Personal Access Tokens → Fine-grained tokens
Click Generate new token
Set:

Token name: api-workshop-lab
Expiration: 7 days
Resource owner: your account
Repository access: Public repositories only
Permissions: Metadata → Read-only (this is the minimum needed)

Click Generate token and copy it immediately — it won’t be shown again
Add it to your .Renviron:

GITHUB_TOKEN=your_token_here






ImportantWhat You Just Did



You created a token with the minimum necessary permissions: read access to metadata on public repositories only. Even if this token leaked, an attacker could only read public data — they could not write code, delete repositories, or access private data. This is the principle of least privilege.




Step 3b — Use the Token

load_dot_env()
github_token <- Sys.getenv("GITHUB_TOKEN")


# Authenticated request — now you get 5000 requests/hour
req <- request("https://api.github.com/user") |>
  req_headers(
    Authorization = glue("Bearer {github_token}"),
    Accept        = "application/vnd.github+json",
    `User-Agent`  = "API-Workshop-Student"
  )

response  <- req_perform(req)
user_data <- resp_body_json(response)

cat("Authenticated as:", user_data$login, "\n")
cat("Rate limit remaining:", resp_header(response, "x-ratelimit-remaining"), "\n")

title: Practice Lab 5: GitHub REST API
text:
Exercise 4: Analyze Your Own Repositories
With the PAT, you can retrieve your own public repository data and build a summary of your GitHub activity.

your_username <- user_data$login

req <- request(glue("https://api.github.com/users/{your_username}/repos")) |>
  req_headers(
    Authorization = glue("Bearer {github_token}"),
    Accept        = "application/vnd.github+json",
    `User-Agent`  = "API-Workshop-Student"
  ) |>
  req_url_query(per_page = 100, sort = "updated")

your_repos <- resp_body_json(req_perform(req))

your_df <- lapply(your_repos, function(r) {
  data.frame(
    name       = r$name,
    language   = r$language %||% "Unknown",
    stars      = r$stargazers_count,
    forks      = r$forks_count,
    is_fork    = r$fork,
    created_at = as.Date(substr(r$created_at, 1, 10))
  )
}) |> bind_rows()

glimpse(your_df)


your_df |>
  filter(!is_fork, language != "Unknown") |>
  count(language, sort = TRUE) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(language, n), y = n, fill = language)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = glue("Repository Languages — {your_username}"),
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Number of Repositories"
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "plasma", end = 0.85) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Practice Lab 5: GitHub REST API
text:
Questions

———————


Q1.
(Conceptual): In Exercise 1, you saw the x-ratelimit-remaining header in the response. Why would an API designer include rate limit information in the response headers rather than (or in addition to) the response body?






TipAnswer Q1





Headers deliver metadata about the response — information about the exchange itself, not the data you requested. Putting rate limit info in headers keeps the response body clean and focused on the requested data. It also means client code can check rate limits without parsing the body — a faster, more efficient check. This is the standard HTTP convention: data goes in the body, metadata goes in headers.




———————


———————



Q2.
(Security): You created a fine-grained token with read-only access to public repositories. Describe a scenario where even this minimal token could be misused if it leaked publicly.






TipAnswer Q2





An attacker with a public-read token could: - Scrape your public repository data at the 5,000 req/hour authenticated rate (vs. 60/hour unauthenticated) — useful for mass enumeration - Identify which public repos you contribute to, revealing professional affiliations or project work you hadn’t publicized - Burn through your rate limit, causing your own API calls to fail
This is why even minimal tokens should be rotated (deleted and regenerated) quickly if leaked, and why expiry dates are important — a 7-day token automatically becomes useless.




———————


———————



Q3.
(Extension): Modify the repository search from Exercise 2 to collect all pages of results (not just the first 20). GitHub limits search results to 1,000 total — use total_count and per_page to calculate how many pages to fetch, and use Sys.sleep(1) between requests to avoid hitting rate limits.






TipAnswer Q3





search_all_github <- function(query, token, max_results = 100) {
  per_page   <- 30
  total_pages <- ceiling(min(max_results, 1000) / per_page)

  all_results <- lapply(seq_len(total_pages), function(p) {
    Sys.sleep(1)  # be a polite client
    req <- request("https://api.github.com/search/repositories") |>
      req_headers(
        Authorization = glue("Bearer {token}"),
        Accept        = "application/vnd.github+json",
        `User-Agent`  = "API-Workshop-Student"
      ) |>
      req_url_query(q = query, sort = "stars", per_page = per_page, page = p)

    data <- resp_body_json(req_perform(req))
    lapply(data$items, function(r) {
      data.frame(name = r$full_name, stars = r$stargazers_count, language = r$language %||% NA)
    }) |> bind_rows()
  }) |> bind_rows()

  all_results
}
Sys.sleep(1) adds a 1-second pause between pages. This is good API citizenship — it avoids triggering abuse detection and spreads requests over time.




———————

title: Practice Lab 1: NASA Open APIs
text:
NASA provides free, production-quality APIs used by researchers worldwide. In this lab you will work with two:

APOD — Astronomy Picture of the Day: one endpoint, one JSON object, a clean introduction.
NEO — Near-Earth Object Web Service: asteroid data with a deeply nested response structure that requires real parsing work.

Auth pattern: API key as a URL query parameter (?api_key=YOUR_KEY).

title: Practice Lab 1: NASA Open APIs
text:
Step 1 — Get Your API Key

Go to api.nasa.gov
Fill in the short registration form and submit
Your key arrives by email within minutes
Add it to your .Renviron file:

NASA_KEY=your_key_here






NoteDEMO_KEY — No signup needed



NASA provides a built-in demo key. Replace nasa_key with "DEMO_KEY" anywhere in this lab. It allows 30 requests/hour and 50/day — enough to complete the exercises.

title: Practice Lab 1: NASA Open APIs
text:
Step 2 — Setup

library(httr2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(glue)
library(dotenv)

load_dot_env()
nasa_key <- Sys.getenv("NASA_KEY")

title: Practice Lab 1: NASA Open APIs
section: Exercise 1: Astronomy Picture of the Day
text:
Exercise 1: Astronomy Picture of the Day
The APOD endpoint returns a single JSON object describing today’s featured image or video, along with a scientific explanation written by a professional astronomer.

apod_url <- glue("https://api.nasa.gov/planetary/apod?api_key={nasa_key}")

req      <- request(apod_url)
response <- req_perform(req)

# Always check the status first
resp_status(response)


apod <- resp_body_json(response)

# Inspect the top-level keys
names(apod)


cat("Title:     ", apod$title,      "\n")
cat("Date:      ", apod$date,       "\n")
cat("Media type:", apod$media_type, "\n")
cat("URL:       ", apod$url,        "\n")
cat("\nExplanation:\n", apod$explanation, "\n")


Challenge A — Request a Specific Date
Add a date query parameter to retrieve any past APOD. Dates go back to 1995-06-16.

apod_dated_url <- glue(
  "https://api.nasa.gov/planetary/apod",
  "?api_key={nasa_key}&date=1995-06-16"
)

apod_first <- resp_body_json(req_perform(request(apod_dated_url)))
cat("First ever APOD:", apod_first$title, "\n")



Challenge B — Retrieve a Batch of APODs
Use the count parameter to retrieve multiple random APODs in a single request.

apod_batch_url <- glue(
  "https://api.nasa.gov/planetary/apod",
  "?api_key={nasa_key}&count=5"
)

apod_list <- resp_body_json(req_perform(request(apod_batch_url)))

# How is the structure different from a single APOD?
length(apod_list)
apod_list[[1]]$title

title: Practice Lab 1: NASA Open APIs
text:
Exercise 2: Near-Earth Object (Asteroid) Data
The NEO endpoint returns every asteroid that passed near Earth within a date range. The response is nested three levels deep — a great exercise in list navigation.

neo_url <- glue(
  "https://api.nasa.gov/neo/rest/v1/feed",
  "?start_date=2024-01-01&end_date=2024-01-07",
  "&api_key={nasa_key}"
)

response <- req_perform(request(neo_url))
neo_data <- resp_body_json(response)

cat("Total asteroids in this range:", neo_data$element_count, "\n")
cat("Top-level keys:", paste(names(neo_data), collapse=", "), "\n")


# Near-Earth objects are nested: neo_data$near_earth_objects → date → list of asteroids
dates <- names(neo_data$near_earth_objects)
cat("Dates in response:", paste(dates, collapse=", "), "\n")

# Look at the first asteroid on the first date
first_day   <- neo_data$near_earth_objects[[dates[1]]]
first_rock  <- first_day[[1]]

cat("Name:", first_rock$name, "\n")
cat("Hazardous?", first_rock$is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid, "\n")
cat("Min diameter (km):",
    first_rock$estimated_diameter$kilometers$estimated_diameter_min, "\n")


# Flatten all dates and asteroids into a single data frame
all_asteroids <- lapply(neo_data$near_earth_objects, function(day) {
  lapply(day, function(rock) {
    data.frame(
      name          = rock$name,
      date          = rock$close_approach_data[[1]]$close_approach_date,
      is_hazardous  = rock$is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid,
      min_diam_km   = rock$estimated_diameter$kilometers$estimated_diameter_min,
      max_diam_km   = rock$estimated_diameter$kilometers$estimated_diameter_max,
      miss_dist_km  = as.numeric(rock$close_approach_data[[1]]$miss_distance$kilometers),
      speed_kmh     = as.numeric(rock$close_approach_data[[1]]$relative_velocity$kilometers_per_hour)
    )
  }) |> bind_rows()
}) |> bind_rows()

glimpse(all_asteroids)


ggplot(all_asteroids, aes(x = miss_dist_km / 1e6, fill = is_hazardous)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, alpha = 0.85, color = "white") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "#4f46e5", "TRUE" = "#ef4444"),
    labels = c("Not Hazardous", "Potentially Hazardous")
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Asteroid Miss Distances — Jan 1–7, 2024",
    subtitle = "Each bar = one asteroid; distance from Earth's center",
    x        = "Miss Distance (millions of km)",
    y        = "Count",
    fill     = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

title: Practice Lab 1: NASA Open APIs
text:
Questions

———————


Q1.
(Conceptual): In this lab the API key is passed as a query parameter (?api_key=...). Name one situation where this approach could create a security risk.






TipAnswer Q1





The key is visible in the URL — which means it appears in browser history, server access logs, and anywhere the URL is copy-pasted or shared. If you accidentally post the URL in a public place (a GitHub issue, a Slack message, a screenshot), your key is exposed. Header-based auth keeps the credential out of the URL entirely.




———————


———————



Q2.
(Code): The count parameter returns a list of APODs instead of a single object. Write code that extracts just the title from each item in the batch and prints them as a character vector.






TipAnswer Q2





titles <- sapply(apod_list, function(x) x$title)
print(titles)
sapply maps a function over each list element and simplifies the result to a vector.




———————


———————



Q3.
(Analysis): After building all_asteroids, use dplyr to find the single asteroid with the smallest miss distance in the date range. Was it potentially hazardous?






TipAnswer Q3





all_asteroids |>
  arrange(miss_dist_km) |>
  select(name, date, miss_dist_km, is_hazardous) |>
  slice(1)
The answer varies by date range, but notice whether is_hazardous is TRUE for the closest pass — this is the heart of what NASA tracks with NEO Watch.




———————

You will use:

title: Practice Lab 3: OpenWeather Extended
text:
You already have your OpenWeather API key from Session 3. This lab takes you further into the same API — exploring endpoints you haven’t used yet and building toward multi-city comparisons.
You will use:

Air Quality Index (AQI) — requires coordinates (lat/lon), not just a city name
5-Day Forecast — 40 time-step nested response across 5 days
Multi-city pipeline — map a function across a vector of cities

Auth pattern: Same as Sessions 3–4 — API key as appid query parameter.

title: Practice Lab 3: OpenWeather Extended
text:
Step 1 — Setup

library(httr2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(glue)
library(dotenv)

load_dot_env()
weather_key <- Sys.getenv("OPENWEATHER_KEY")

title: Practice Lab 3: OpenWeather Extended
text:
Exercise 1: Air Quality Index
The AQI endpoint requires latitude and longitude, not a city name. This means you need to geocode first — exactly the pattern from Session 3.

Step 1a — Geocode a city

get_coordinates <- function(city, key) {
  url <- glue("http://api.openweathermap.org/geo/1.0/direct?q={city}&limit=1&appid={key}")
  result <- resp_body_json(req_perform(request(url)))
  list(lat = result[[1]]$lat, lon = result[[1]]$lon)
}

coords <- get_coordinates("San Luis Obispo", weather_key)
cat("Lat:", coords$lat, "| Lon:", coords$lon, "\n")



Step 1b — Fetch air quality

aqi_url <- glue(
  "http://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/air_pollution",
  "?lat={coords$lat}&lon={coords$lon}&appid={weather_key}"
)

response <- req_perform(request(aqi_url))
aqi_data <- resp_body_json(response)

# AQI scale: 1=Good, 2=Fair, 3=Moderate, 4=Poor, 5=Very Poor
aqi_value  <- aqi_data$list[[1]]$main$aqi
components <- aqi_data$list[[1]]$components

cat("Air Quality Index:", aqi_value, "\n")
cat("PM2.5:", components$pm2_5, "μg/m³\n")
cat("CO:",    components$co,    "μg/m³\n")
cat("NO2:",   components$no2,   "μg/m³\n")
cat("O3:",    components$o3,    "μg/m³\n")

title: Practice Lab 3: OpenWeather Extended
text:
Exercise 2: 5-Day Forecast
The forecast endpoint returns 40 time steps — one every 3 hours over 5 days. The data lives inside a list key (an array).

forecast_url <- glue(
  "https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/forecast",
  "?q=San+Luis+Obispo&appid={weather_key}&units=imperial"
)

response      <- req_perform(request(forecast_url))
forecast_data <- resp_body_json(response)

cat("Number of time steps:", length(forecast_data$list), "\n")
cat("City:", forecast_data$city$name, ",", forecast_data$city$country, "\n")


# Flatten the nested list into a tidy data frame
forecast_df <- lapply(forecast_data$list, function(step) {
  data.frame(
    datetime    = as_datetime(step$dt),
    temp        = step$main$temp,
    feels_like  = step$main$feels_like,
    humidity    = step$main$humidity,
    description = step$weather[[1]]$description,
    wind_speed  = step$wind$speed,
    pop         = step$pop  # probability of precipitation
  )
}) |> bind_rows()

glimpse(forecast_df)


ggplot(forecast_df, aes(x = datetime, y = temp)) +
  geom_line(color = "#4f46e5", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = temp - 3, ymax = temp + 3), alpha = 0.15, fill = "#6366f1") +
  geom_point(aes(color = pop), size = 2) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "#a5b4fc", high = "#ef4444",
                       name = "Rain Prob.") +
  labs(
    title    = "5-Day Temperature Forecast — San Luis Obispo",
    subtitle = "Point color indicates probability of precipitation",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Temperature (°F)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Practice Lab 3: OpenWeather Extended
text:
Exercise 3: Compare Multiple Cities
Write a reusable function, then map it across a list of cities. This is the core pattern for any multi-source data pipeline.

get_current_weather <- function(city, key) {
  url <- glue(
    "https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather",
    "?q={city}&appid={key}&units=imperial"
  )

  response <- req_perform(request(url))

  if (resp_status(response) != 200) {
    message("Failed for city: ", city)
    return(NULL)
  }

  d <- resp_body_json(response)

  data.frame(
    city        = d$name,
    country     = d$sys$country,
    temp        = d$main$temp,
    feels_like  = d$main$feels_like,
    humidity    = d$main$humidity,
    description = d$weather[[1]]$description,
    wind_speed  = d$wind$speed
  )
}


cities <- c(
  "San Luis Obispo,US",
  "Chicago,US",
  "Miami,US",
  "Seattle,US",
  "Denver,US"
)

# Map the function across all cities
weather_comparison <- lapply(cities, get_current_weather, key = weather_key) |>
  bind_rows()

print(weather_comparison)


ggplot(weather_comparison, aes(x = reorder(city, temp), y = temp, fill = temp)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, radius = unit(4, "pt")) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(temp), "°F")),
            hjust = -0.15, fontface = "bold", size = 4) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#4f46e5", high = "#ef4444") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Current Temperature by City",
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Temperature (°F)",
    fill  = "Temp (°F)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Practice Lab 3: OpenWeather Extended
text:
Questions

———————


Q1.
(Conceptual): The AQI endpoint requires lat/lon, not a city name. Why do you think some API endpoints work with city names while others require coordinates?






TipAnswer Q1





City names are ambiguous — there are many cities named “Springfield” or “Paris.” Coordinates are globally unique. Endpoints that measure physical phenomena at a precise geographic point (like air quality or elevation) require coordinates because a city-level approximation would be inaccurate. The geocoding step converts the human-readable name into the precise coordinates the scientific endpoint needs.




———————


———————



Q2.
(Code): The 5-day forecast datetime column comes from a Unix timestamp (step$dt). What does as_datetime() from lubridate do to it, and what would the raw value look like without that conversion?






TipAnswer Q2





as_datetime() converts the raw Unix timestamp (an integer counting seconds since January 1, 1970) into a human-readable POSIXct datetime object. Without conversion, the column would contain numbers like 1704067200 — correct but unreadable and unusable for time-based plotting.




———————


———————



Q3.
(Extension): Modify get_current_weather() to also retrieve the air quality index for each city. You’ll need to geocode the city first, then call the AQI endpoint with the coordinates. What challenges do you run into?






TipAnswer Q3





The main challenge is making two API calls per city instead of one — geocoding (to get lat/lon) and then AQI (using those coordinates). This doubles the number of requests and makes rate limiting more of a concern. A robust solution caches the coordinates and handles errors at each step:
get_weather_and_aqi <- function(city, key) {
  coords <- get_coordinates(city, key)
  weather <- get_current_weather(city, key)

  aqi_url  <- glue("http://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/air_pollution?lat={coords$lat}&lon={coords$lon}&appid={key}")
  aqi_resp <- resp_body_json(req_perform(request(aqi_url)))

  weather$aqi <- aqi_resp$list[[1]]$main$aqi
  weather
}




———————

title: Practice Lab 2: The Cat API
text:
The Cat API is deliberately low-stakes — but it teaches something important. Unlike NASA (key in the URL), this API expects your credential in an HTTP request header (x-api-key). That one change in technique is the key learning of this lab.
You’ll also see what happens when you make the same request with and without auth — the API returns different amounts of data, making the difference tangible.
Auth pattern: API key in a request header (x-api-key: YOUR_KEY).

title: Practice Lab 2: The Cat API
text:
The Cat API is deliberately low-stakes — but it teaches something important. Unlike NASA (key in the URL), this API expects your credential in an HTTP request header (x-api-key). That one change in technique is the key learning of this lab.
You’ll also see what happens when you make the same request with and without auth — the API returns different amounts of data, making the difference tangible.
Auth pattern: API key in a request header (x-api-key: YOUR_KEY).

title: Practice Lab 2: The Cat API
text:
Step 1 — Get Your API Key

Go to thecatapi.com and click Get Your API Key
Enter your email — the key arrives instantly
Add it to your .Renviron file:

CAT_KEY=your_key_here






NoteNo Key? No Problem — For Now



The Cat API works without a key for basic endpoints, returning a small subset of data. This is intentional — you’ll use the unauthenticated version first in Exercise 1 to see the contrast.

title: Practice Lab 2: The Cat API
text:
Step 2 — Setup

library(httr2)
library(dplyr)
library(glue)
library(dotenv)

load_dot_env()
cat_key <- Sys.getenv("CAT_KEY")

title: Practice Lab 2: The Cat API
section: Exercise 1: Authenticated vs. Unauthenticated — See the Difference
text:
Exercise 1: Authenticated vs. Unauthenticated — See the Difference
This exercise makes the value of authentication concrete by running the same request both ways.

Without a key (unauthenticated)

req_no_auth <- request("https://api.thecatapi.com/v1/images/search")
response_no_auth <- req_perform(req_no_auth)

result_no_auth <- resp_body_json(response_no_auth)

cat("Fields returned (no auth):", paste(names(result_no_auth[[1]]), collapse=", "), "\n")
cat("Number of results:", length(result_no_auth), "\n")



With a key (authenticated)
Notice the only change is the addition of req_headers().

req_auth <- request("https://api.thecatapi.com/v1/images/search") |>
  req_headers("x-api-key" = cat_key)

response_auth <- req_perform(req_auth)
result_auth   <- resp_body_json(response_auth)

cat("Fields returned (with auth):", paste(names(result_auth[[1]]), collapse=", "), "\n")







ImportantWhat changed?



Look at the field lists side by side. Authenticated responses include breeds, categories, and breed_ids — fields that are gated behind auth. This is a common API design pattern: basic access is free, richer data requires a credential.

title: Practice Lab 2: The Cat API
section: Exercise 2: Search for Images with Limit & Size
text:
Exercise 2: Search for Images with Limit & Size

req <- request("https://api.thecatapi.com/v1/images/search") |>
  req_headers("x-api-key" = cat_key) |>
  req_url_query(limit = 10, size = "med")

response <- req_perform(req)
images   <- resp_body_json(response)

cat("Number of images returned:", length(images), "\n")

# Extract URLs
urls <- sapply(images, function(img) img$url)
print(urls)

title: Practice Lab 2: The Cat API
text:
Exercise 3: Retrieve Breed Information
The Cat API has a /breeds endpoint that returns a list of all cat breeds with detailed attributes.

req <- request("https://api.thecatapi.com/v1/breeds") |>
  req_headers("x-api-key" = cat_key)

response <- req_perform(req)
breeds   <- resp_body_json(response)

cat("Total breeds available:", length(breeds), "\n")
cat("Fields per breed:", paste(names(breeds[[1]]), collapse=", "), "\n")


# Flatten into a data frame
breed_df <- lapply(breeds, function(b) {
  data.frame(
    name              = b$name,
    origin            = b$origin %||% NA_character_,
    life_span         = b$life_span %||% NA_character_,
    temperament       = b$temperament %||% NA_character_,
    intelligence      = b$intelligence %||% NA_integer_,
    affection_level   = b$affection_level %||% NA_integer_,
    energy_level      = b$energy_level %||% NA_integer_,
    stringsAsFactors  = FALSE
  )
}) |> bind_rows()

glimpse(breed_df)


# What are the most intelligent breeds?
breed_df |>
  arrange(desc(intelligence)) |>
  select(name, origin, intelligence, energy_level, affection_level) |>
  head(10)

title: Practice Lab 2: The Cat API
section: Exercise 4: Search for Images of a Specific Breed
text:
Exercise 4: Search for Images of a Specific Breed
Combine what you’ve learned — use a breed ID from Exercise 3 to search for images of that specific breed.

# Get the breed ID for Abyssinian
abyssinian_id <- breed_df |>
  filter(name == "Abyssinian") |>
  pull(name)

# Use the breeds endpoint to find the actual ID
abyssinian_entry <- breeds[[which(sapply(breeds, function(b) b$name) == "Abyssinian")]]
breed_id <- abyssinian_entry$id

cat("Breed ID:", breed_id, "\n")

# Search for images of that breed
req <- request("https://api.thecatapi.com/v1/images/search") |>
  req_headers("x-api-key" = cat_key) |>
  req_url_query(breed_ids = breed_id, limit = 5)

breed_images <- resp_body_json(req_perform(req))
cat("Images found:", length(breed_images), "\n")
sapply(breed_images, function(img) img$url)

title: Practice Lab 2: The Cat API
text:
Questions

———————


Q1.
(Conceptual): In Exercise 1, you ran the same endpoint twice — once without a key and once with one. The URL was identical both times. Where exactly did the credential travel in the authenticated request?






TipAnswer Q1





The credential traveled in the HTTP request header — specifically the x-api-key header. It is not part of the URL, query string, or response body. Headers are transmitted in the request metadata, which is separate from the URL and typically not logged in the same places a URL would be.




———————


———————



Q2.
(Code — Callback to Lab 1): In Lab 1 (NASA), the API key was passed as ?api_key=KEY in the URL. Rewrite the unauthenticated Cat API request from Exercise 1 as if the Cat API used a query parameter instead of a header. What would that look like using req_url_query()?






TipAnswer Q2





req_param_style <- request("https://api.thecatapi.com/v1/images/search") |>
  req_url_query(api_key = cat_key)
This is a hypothetical — the Cat API doesn’t actually support this. But the exercise shows that req_url_query() and req_headers() are the two different tools for two different auth patterns, and that the API documentation tells you which one to use.




———————


———————



Q3.
(Analysis): Using breed_df, which origin country has the highest average intelligence score across all its breeds? Use dplyr to find out.






TipAnswer Q3





breed_df |>
  filter(!is.na(intelligence), !is.na(origin)) |>
  group_by(origin) |>
  summarise(avg_intelligence = mean(intelligence, na.rm = TRUE), count = n()) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_intelligence)) |>
  head(5)
Countries with only one or two breeds may score high just by chance — look at the count column alongside the average.




———————

title: Practice Lab 4: TMDB Movie Database
text:
TMDB (The Movie Database) is a community-built database of movies, TV shows, actors, and genres. It is an excellent API for learning because it supports two authentication methods simultaneously — you’ll use both.

API Key — passed as a query parameter, same pattern as NASA
Bearer Token — passed in the Authorization header, the industry-standard modern approach

Understanding Bearer tokens is critical: every major professional API (Spotify, Stripe, GitHub, Google Cloud) uses this pattern.
Auth patterns: API key in URL and Bearer token in header.

title: Practice Lab 4: TMDB Movie Database
text:
TMDB (The Movie Database) is a community-built database of movies, TV shows, actors, and genres. It is an excellent API for learning because it supports two authentication methods simultaneously — you’ll use both.

API Key — passed as a query parameter, same pattern as NASA
Bearer Token — passed in the Authorization header, the industry-standard modern approach

Understanding Bearer tokens is critical: every major professional API (Spotify, Stripe, GitHub, Google Cloud) uses this pattern.
Auth patterns: API key in URL and Bearer token in header.

title: Practice Lab 4: TMDB Movie Database
text:
Step 1 — Get Your Credentials

Go to themoviedb.org and create a free account
Go to Settings → API (left sidebar)
Request an API key — select “Developer” and fill in the form
On the same page you will see:

API Key (v3) — a 32-character hex string
Read Access Token (v4 auth) — a long JWT-style Bearer token

Add both to your .Renviron:

TMDB_KEY=your_api_key_here
TMDB_TOKEN=your_read_access_token_here

title: Practice Lab 4: TMDB Movie Database
text:
Step 2 — Setup

library(httr2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(glue)
library(dotenv)

load_dot_env()
tmdb_key   <- Sys.getenv("TMDB_KEY")
tmdb_token <- Sys.getenv("TMDB_TOKEN")

title: Practice Lab 4: TMDB Movie Database
section: Exercise 1: Search Movies Using an API Key
text:
Exercise 1: Search Movies Using an API Key
This is the simplest auth pattern — identical to how you used the NASA and OpenWeather APIs.

req <- request("https://api.themoviedb.org/3/search/movie") |>
  req_url_query(
    api_key  = tmdb_key,
    query    = "Interstellar",
    language = "en-US",
    page     = 1
  )

response <- req_perform(req)
search_data <- resp_body_json(response)

cat("Total results:", search_data$total_results, "\n")
cat("Pages:", search_data$total_pages, "\n")


# Flatten the results array
movies_df <- lapply(search_data$results, function(m) {
  data.frame(
    id            = m$id,
    title         = m$title,
    release_date  = m$release_date %||% NA_character_,
    vote_average  = m$vote_average,
    vote_count    = m$vote_count,
    popularity    = m$popularity,
    overview      = substr(m$overview %||% "", 1, 100)
  )
}) |> bind_rows()

movies_df |> select(title, release_date, vote_average, popularity)

title: Practice Lab 4: TMDB Movie Database
section: Exercise 2: Get Popular Movies Using a Bearer Token
text:
Exercise 2: Get Popular Movies Using a Bearer Token
Now use the Bearer token. The endpoint is the same family, but the credential moves from the URL to an Authorization header.

req <- request("https://api.themoviedb.org/3/movie/popular") |>
  req_headers(
    Authorization = glue("Bearer {tmdb_token}"),
    Accept        = "application/json"
  ) |>
  req_url_query(language = "en-US", page = 1)

response   <- req_perform(req)
pop_data   <- resp_body_json(response)

popular_df <- lapply(pop_data$results, function(m) {
  data.frame(
    title        = m$title,
    release_date = m$release_date %||% NA_character_,
    vote_average = m$vote_average,
    popularity   = m$popularity,
    genre_ids    = paste(unlist(m$genre_ids), collapse = ",")
  )
}) |> bind_rows()

head(popular_df, 10)







ImportantComparing the Two Approaches



Notice what changed: with the API key, credentials were in the URL (?api_key=...). With the Bearer token, the URL is clean and the credential is in the Authorization header. The response structure is identical — only the auth mechanism differs. This is exactly the difference you saw between the NASA lab and the Cat API lab.

Each lab targets a different API and introduces a new concept in credential handling, authentication, or response structure. The labs are self-contained — students can work through any of them after completing the core workshop sessions.

Lab Sequencing & Pedagogy

Lab

title: Instructor Guide — Practice Labs
text:
Lab 1 — NASA Open APIs

Learning Objectives

Retrieve data from a real scientific API
Practice GET requests with API key as a query parameter
Work with deeply nested JSON (NEO asteroid data)



Setup Time
~5 minutes. Students sign up at api.nasa.gov — the key arrives by email immediately. Alternatively, DEMO_KEY works out of the box (30 requests/hour limit).


Key Teaching Points

The API key goes in the URL as a query parameter (?api_key=...)
APOD returns a single JSON object; NEO returns a multi-level nested structure
The NEO response requires iteration (lapply) to flatten before analysis



Common Errors








Error
Likely Cause
Fix




403 Forbidden
DEMO_KEY rate limit hit
Wait 1 hour, or register for a free personal key


400 Bad Request on NEO
Date range exceeds 7 days
Shorten the range to ≤ 7 days


Empty $url in APOD
Media type is video, not image
Check apod$media_type first





Discussion Prompt

“The key is in the URL. What happens to security when you share that URL with someone else?”

title: Instructor Guide — Practice Labs
text:
Lab 2 — The Cat API

Learning Objectives

Pass credentials in an HTTP header instead of the URL
Understand why header-based auth is more secure
Practice req_headers() in httr2



Setup Time
~3 minutes. Free API key at thecatapi.com. The API works without a key for basic endpoints, which is useful for demonstrating the difference between authenticated and unauthenticated requests in class.


Key Teaching Points

The x-api-key header is a custom header — not all APIs use the same header name
Header contents are not visible in the URL or browser history
Unauthenticated requests return a smaller subset of results — a good live demo



Common Errors








Error
Likely Cause
Fix




Empty results on /votes
These endpoints require auth
Confirm the header is being set


401 on favorite endpoints
Key not passed or malformed
Print req object before performing to inspect headers





Discussion Prompt

“Why would an API designer choose headers over query parameters for credentials? What does the URL look like in each case?”



Classroom Demo
Run the breed search request twice — once with the header and once without. Show students the difference in the response payload (authenticated responses include breed_temperament and other premium fields).

title: Instructor Guide — Practice Labs
text:
Lab 3 — OpenWeather (Extended)

Learning Objectives

Apply skills from Sessions 3–4 to new endpoints (Air Quality, Forecast)
Compare data across multiple cities using iteration
Explore multi-call workflows



Setup Time
None additional — students already have their OpenWeather API key from Session 3.


Key Teaching Points

The Air Quality Index endpoint requires coordinates (lat/lon), not a city name — reinforcing geocoding from Session 3
The 5-day forecast response nests list (an array of 40 time-step objects) — good practice at .[[1]] indexing
Comparing cities with lapply introduces the pattern of writing a function once and mapping it



Common Errors








Error
Likely Cause
Fix




401 on all calls
Key not yet activated (takes up to 2 hours after signup)
Use a pre-made key for class; students activate theirs at home


Empty list in forecast
Wrong response path — use $list not $forecast
names(resp_body_json(response)) to inspect top-level keys





Discussion Prompt

“The forecast endpoint returns 40 time steps (3-hour intervals over 5 days). What would a tidy data frame of this look like, and how would you get there?”

title: Instructor Guide — Practice Labs
text:
Lab 4 — TMDB Movie Database

Learning Objectives

Authenticate with both an API key and a Bearer token
Understand the difference between the two approaches
Recognize the Authorization: Bearer ... pattern used in professional APIs



Setup Time
~8 minutes. Students register at themoviedb.org, go to Settings → API, and receive both an API Key (v3) and a Read Access Token (v4 Bearer). Both are on the same settings page.


Key Teaching Points

The API Key goes in the URL (?api_key=...) — same pattern as NASA
The Bearer token goes in the Authorization header (Authorization: Bearer TOKEN)
Bearer tokens are the industry standard in modern APIs (OAuth 2.0 pattern)
TMDB’s /search/movie endpoint introduces pagination via ?page=



Common Errors








Error
Likely Cause
Fix




401 with Bearer token
Token copied with extra whitespace
Re-copy and check nchar(token)


Empty results array
Search query URL-encoded incorrectly
Use req_url_query(query = "movie name") instead of manual encoding


404 on endpoint
Wrong API version — v3 vs v4
Confirm endpoint starts with /3/ for v3 key auth





Discussion Prompt

“You’ve now used three auth methods: query param (NASA, TMDB key), custom header (Cat API x-api-key), and Authorization Bearer (TMDB token). As a developer choosing how to secure an API, what would drive that decision?”

title: Instructor Guide — Practice Labs
text:
Lab 5 — GitHub REST API

Learning Objectives

Make unauthenticated requests to a public API
Create and use a Personal Access Token (PAT) with scoped permissions
Understand the principle of least-privilege in API authorization



Setup Time

Unauthenticated exercises: zero setup
PAT creation: ~5 minutes at github.com → Settings → Developer Settings → Fine-grained tokens



Key Teaching Points

GitHub’s public endpoints work without any authentication — good entry point
Rate limits differ significantly: 60 req/hour unauthenticated vs. 5,000 req/hour with a token
Fine-grained tokens let students explicitly choose what permissions to grant — a live demonstration of least-privilege security
The User-Agent header is required by GitHub’s API



Common Errors








Error
Likely Cause
Fix




403 or requires authentication
Endpoint requires auth (e.g., private repos)
Switch to PAT-authenticated request


422 Unprocessable Entity on search
Query syntax error
Check GitHub search syntax — strings must be URL-encoded


401 with PAT
Token expired or wrong scope
Check token expiry date and permissions in GitHub settings





Discussion Prompt

“You created a fine-grained token that can only read public repositories. If that token were accidentally leaked in a public repo, what damage could an attacker do with it? What if it had ‘write’ permissions?”



Classroom Demo
Search for repositories with the topic "tidyverse" using the GitHub search API. Shows students how to discover open-source projects programmatically — a direct real-world use case.

title: Instructor Guide — Practice Labs
text:
General Troubleshooting

Diagnosing Any API Error
Teach students this four-step debugging process:
# Step 1: Check the status code
resp_status(response)

# Step 2: Read the error message body
resp_body_string(response)

# Step 3: Inspect the request that was sent
req  # print the request object to see URL, headers

# Step 4: Check the API documentation for that status code


Rate Limits
All free-tier APIs in these labs have rate limits. If a class of 30 students all hit an API simultaneously, shared IP limits can trigger 429 Too Many Requests. Strategies:

Use staggered start times (group 1 starts on NASA, group 2 starts on Cat API)
Each student should use their own API key, not a shared one
Add Sys.sleep(1) between repeated calls in loop exercises



.Renviron Pattern (Remind Students)
All labs expect keys to be stored in .Renviron and loaded with dotenv::load_dot_env() or readRenviron("~/.Renviron").
# In .Renviron (never commit this file):
NASA_KEY=your_nasa_key_here
CAT_KEY=your_cat_api_key_here
TMDB_KEY=your_tmdb_api_key_here
TMDB_TOKEN=your_tmdb_bearer_token_here
GITHUB_TOKEN=your_github_pat_here

# In R:
dotenv::load_dot_env()
nasa_key <- Sys.getenv("NASA_KEY")

title: Instructor Guide — Practice Labs
text:
Suggested Follow-Up Projects
After completing the labs, students who want a project can:

Dashboard project: Pull NASA APOD for the past 30 days, extract keywords from the title/explanation using stringr, and visualize the most common astronomy topics.
Movie recommender: Use TMDB to build a genre-based recommendation table — given a genre, return the top 10 highest-rated movies of the current year.
City comparison: Use the extended OpenWeather lab to compare air quality and temperature trends for 5 cities over the past week.
GitHub portfolio analyzer: Use the GitHub API to retrieve your own repositories and visualize your commit activity and language distribution.

title: Session 4: Historical Weather, Visualization & Wrap-Up
text:
In Session 3, we:

Set up our API key securely using .Renviron
Made our first real request to the OpenWeather Current Weather API
Built a geocode() function to convert city names to coordinates
Practiced making our own requests

Now we’ll combine geocoding with the One Call API to get historical weather data, loop over multiple days, and create visualizations.

In Session 3, we:

Set up our API key securely using .Renviron
Made our first real request to the OpenWeather Current Weather API

Practiced making our own requests

Now we’ll combine geocoding with the One Call API to get historical weather data, loop over multiple days, and create visualizations.

title: Session 4: Historical Weather, Visualization & Wrap-Up
text:
Part 1: Getting Historical Weather Data
The OpenWeather One Call API 3.0 provides historical daily summaries for a given latitude, longitude, and date. This requires coordinates — which is why we built the geocode() function!

The Plan

Use the Geocoding API to get lat/lon for a city
Define a range of past dates
Loop over each date, calling the One Call API
Combine all results into a single data frame



Load Libraries

library(httr2)
library(tibble)
library(lubridate)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(dotenv)
library(glue)


dotenv::load_dot_env(file = ".Renviron.txt")



Step-by-Step: Past 5 Days of Weather

# Step 1: Choose city and number of days
city_name <- "San Luis Obispo"
numdays <- 5


# Step 2: Geocode the city
geo_url <- glue(
  "http://api.openweathermap.org/geo/1.0/direct?",
  "q=", URLencode(city_name),
  "&limit=1&appid=", Sys.getenv("API_KEY")
)

geo_response <- req_perform(request(geo_url))

if (resp_status(geo_response) == 200) {
  geo_data_df <- resp_body_json(geo_response) |>
    as.data.frame()
  
  if (length(geo_data_df) == 0) {
    stop("City not found. Please check the city name.")
  }
  
  lat <- round(geo_data_df$lat, digits = 2)
  lon <- round(geo_data_df$lon, digits = 2)
  
  cat("Coordinates for", city_name, "-> Lat:", lat, "Lon:", lon, "\n")
}


# Step 3: Define date range
date_range <- as.character(lubridate::today() - days(1:numdays))
print(date_range)







NoteWhy as.character()?



Without wrapping in as.character(), dates will mathematically evaluate and become integers when used in URL construction. We need them as strings like "2026-08-05" for the API.



# Step 4: Loop over dates and call the API
hist_weather_df <- data.frame()

for (date in date_range) {
  
  weather_url <- glue(
    "https://api.openweathermap.org/data/3.0/onecall/day_summary?",
    "lat=", lat,
    "&lon=", lon,
    "&date=", date,
    "&appid=", Sys.getenv("API_KEY"),
    "&units=imperial"
  )
  
  weather_response <- req_perform(request(weather_url))
  
  if (resp_status(weather_response) == 200) {
    daily_weather_df <- resp_body_json(weather_response) |>
      as.data.frame()
    
    # Add city and date columns
    daily_weather_df <- daily_weather_df |>
      mutate(
        city = city_name,
        date = date
      )
    
    hist_weather_df <- bind_rows(hist_weather_df, daily_weather_df)
    
  } else {
    warning(paste("Failed to get weather for", date, "-", resp_status(weather_response)))
  }
}

print(hist_weather_df)



Understanding the Response Structure
The API returns a JSON object representing summary statistics for a specific location and date. The nested structure flattens into descriptive column names:









JSON Key
R Column Name
Type
Description




temperature.min
temperature.min
numeric
Minimum temp for the day


temperature.max
temperature.max
numeric
Maximum temp for the day


wind.max.speed
wind.max.speed
numeric
Peak wind speed


date (added)
date
character
From our loop variable


city (added)
city
character
City name from geocoding



Example row:



temperature.min
temperature.max
wind.max.speed
date
city




50.1
75.6
12.3
2026-08-04
San Luis Obispo




———————



Q1.
(Open-Ended): In the loop above, we use bind_rows() to combine data from each day. Why do we initialize hist_weather_df <- data.frame() before the loop? What would happen if we didn’t?






TipAnswer Q1





We initialize an empty data frame before the loop so that bind_rows() has something to append to on the first iteration. Without it, R would throw an error because hist_weather_df wouldn’t exist when we try to bind the first day’s results to it. This is a common pattern in R: initialize → loop → accumulate.




———————


———————



Q2.
(Multiple Choice — Callback to Session 1 Q5): In the loop, we use warning() instead of stop() when a single day’s request fails. Why?

warning() is faster than stop()
warning() lets the loop continue to the next date, while stop() would halt everything
warning() sends a message to the API server
There is no practical difference







TipAnswer Q2





b) Using warning() lets the loop continue processing remaining dates even if one fails. If we used stop(), the entire function would halt at the first failure — losing all data for subsequent dates.




———————

title: Session 4: Historical Weather, Visualization & Wrap-Up
Session 4: Historical Weather, Visualization & Wrap-Up
text:
Part 3: Visualizing Weather Data
Now let’s make the data come alive with visualizations!

Temperature Over Time

# Get data
df4 <- prev_weather("Ames, IA, USA", 4) |>
  mutate(date = as.Date(date))

# Plot high and low temperatures
ggplot(df4, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = temperature.max, color = "High"), linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = temperature.min, color = "Low"), linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("High" = "red", "Low" = "blue")) +
  labs(
    title = paste("High and Low Temperatures in", unique(df4$city)),
    x = "Date",
    y = "Temperature (°F)",
    color = "Temperature Type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)



Multi-City Comparison Map
Let’s compare weather across multiple cities using a map:

library(sf)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)


cities <- c("San Luis Obispo", "Santa Barbara", "San Francisco")

# Function to get coordinates + weather summary for a single city
get_city_weather <- function(city, date = Sys.Date()) {
  geo_url <- glue(
    "http://api.openweathermap.org/geo/1.0/direct?",
    "q=", URLencode(city),
    "&limit=1&appid=", Sys.getenv("API_KEY")
  )
  
  geo_response <- req_perform(request(geo_url))
  
  if (resp_status(geo_response) == 200) {
    geo_data_df <- as.data.frame(resp_body_json(geo_response))
    
    if (length(geo_data_df) == 0) return(NULL)
    
    lat <- geo_data_df$lat
    lon <- geo_data_df$lon
    
    weather_url <- glue(
      "https://api.openweathermap.org/data/3.0/onecall/day_summary?",
      "lat=", lat,
      "&lon=", lon,
      "&date=", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
      "&appid=", Sys.getenv("API_KEY"),
      "&units=imperial"
    )
    
    weather_response <- req_perform(request(weather_url))
    
    if (resp_status(weather_response) == 200) {
      weather_data <- resp_body_json(weather_response)
      
      tibble(
        city = city,
        date = date,
        lat = lat,
        lon = lon,
        temp_max = weather_data$temperature$max,
        temp_min = weather_data$temperature$min
      )
    } else return(NULL)
  } else return(NULL)
}

# Fetch for all cities
weather_df <- bind_rows(lapply(cities, get_city_weather))


# Convert to spatial data
weather_sf <- weather_df |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)

# Get California boundary
ca <- ne_states(country = "United States of America", returnclass = "sf") |>
  filter(name == "California")

# Plot
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = ca, fill = "gray95", color = "gray60") +
  geom_sf(data = weather_sf, aes(color = temp_max), size = 6) +
  geom_sf_label(data = weather_sf, aes(label = city), nudge_y = 0.5, size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "High Temp (°F)") +
  labs(
    title = "California City Temperatures",
    subtitle = paste("Date:", Sys.Date())
  ) +
  theme_minimal()


———————



Q4.
(Open-Ended): We used lapply(cities, get_city_weather) to apply the function to multiple cities. How is this different from using a for loop? When might you prefer one over the other?






TipAnswer Q4





lapply() applies a function to each element of a list and returns a list of results — it’s a more “functional programming” approach. A for loop does the same thing but requires manually initializing and accumulating results. lapply() is more concise and idiomatic in R. However, for loops can be easier to read and debug, especially when you need to handle errors or track progress. For API work with potential failures, a for loop with tryCatch or warning() can be more transparent.




———————
